a.name: Consequentialism
a.oneLiner: An act is right insofar as it produces the best overall outcomes for everyone affected.

b.name: Deontology
b.oneLiner: Some acts are required or forbidden in themselves, whatever good their violation might buy.

pairs.splitHint: They diverge the moment a policy helps many by wronging a few: the consequentialist runs the arithmetic, the deontologist rules the trade off the table.

a.name: Moral universalism
a.oneLiner: Some moral standards bind every society, and a practice can be wrong even where it is customary.

b.name: Moral relativism
b.oneLiner: Moral standards are internal to cultures, and judging one society by another's code is a confusion.

pairs.splitHint: They split over cross-border judgment: whether condemning another culture's practice is moral seriousness or moral imperialism.

a.name: Psychological egoism
a.oneLiner: All human action, however generous it looks, is ultimately moved by self-interest.

b.name: Genuine altruism
b.oneLiner: People sometimes act for another's sake, full stop — the giving is not a disguised purchase.

pairs.splitHint: They diverge on how to read a conspicuous good deed: as reputation, tax, and self-image doing the real work, or as evidence that concern for others can be the engine itself.

a.name: Inductive confidence
a.oneLiner: Past regularities rationally license forecasts — models and track records are how knowledge of the future works.

b.name: Inductive skepticism
b.oneLiner: No amount of past pattern guarantees the next case, and confidence in extrapolation outruns its warrant.

pairs.splitHint: They diverge over what a model's past success actually proves: whether a strong track record justifies acting on the forecast, or merely reports that the surprise hasn't arrived yet.

a.name: Hard determinism
a.oneLiner: Every choice is the outcome of prior causes, so no one is ultimately responsible in the way blame assumes.

b.name: Libertarian free will
b.oneLiner: Agents genuinely could have done otherwise, and that open alternative is what makes praise and blame apt.

pairs.splitHint: They diverge at the courtroom door: whether a wrongdoer's biography and biology excuse, mitigate, or leave untouched the claim that they could have chosen differently.

a.name: Machine thought is possible
a.oneLiner: Thinking is what a system does, not what it is made of — the right functioning would be real thought.

b.name: Machine thought is impossible
b.oneLiner: However fluent the output, symbol manipulation without understanding is imitation, not thought.

pairs.splitHint: They diverge on whether impressive machine performance is evidence of a mind or only of mimicry — one asks what the system does, the other what doing it is like from inside.

a.name: Liberty first
a.oneLiner: The state's first duty is to leave people free; coercion needs justification, freedom does not.

b.name: Equality first
b.oneLiner: Freedom that only some can afford is a privilege wearing liberty's name; fair shares come first.

pairs.splitHint: They diverge whenever a rule restricts choice to level the field: one sees liberty taxed, the other sees liberty finally being distributed.

a.name: Consent of the governed
a.oneLiner: Political power is legitimate only insofar as those subject to it have genuinely consented.

b.name: Utilitarian statecraft
b.oneLiner: Institutions are justified by the welfare they produce, whether or not anyone signed anything.

pairs.splitHint: They diverge when an unpopular measure works: one asks whether the governed agreed to it, the other asks only whether it left them better off.

a.name: Art's moral accountability
a.oneLiner: Art acts on its audience and its subjects, and can be judged — even removed — on moral grounds.

b.name: Aesthetic autonomy
b.oneLiner: A work's value as art is one thing and its morals another; conflating them judges the wrong object.

pairs.splitHint: They diverge at every cancellation and every museum wall label: whether a work's moral failings count against the art itself or only against something adjacent to it.
